brief_document.title: aion-store crate, EventStore trait, and StoreError
brief_document.purpose: Define the single persistence contract for Aion. The EventStore trait is what the engine talks to and what every backend implements, including external ones.
brief_document.task: Create the aion-store leaf crate depending only on aion-core. Define StoreError as a closed enum. Define the EventStore async trait with append (expected-sequence guard), read_history, list_active, query, schedule_timer, and expired_timers. Define the TimerEntry type the timer methods exchange.

requirements.title: Crate scaffold and StoreError
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL create the aion-store crate depending only on aion-core (no other Aion crate). THE SYSTEM SHALL define StoreError as a closed enum with SequenceConflict, NotFound, Backend, and Serialization variants.
acceptance: crates/aion-store exists, is a workspace member, and depends on aion-core plus external crates only (async-trait or native async, thiserror, serde).
src/lib.rs contains only declarations and re-exports.
StoreError has variants SequenceConflict { expected, found }, NotFound { workflow_id }, Backend(String or boxed error), Serialization(String).
StoreError derives Debug, implements std::error::Error and Display, and is Send + Sync.

A doc comment on StoreError::NotFound states it is reserved for operations that target a must-exist workflow; the read and query methods defined in this crate return empty results (not NotFound) for absent workflows.

requirements.title: EventStore trait — event methods
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define EventStore as an async trait that is Send + Sync + 'static, with append (taking an expected sequence and applying events atomically), read_history, list_active, and query.
acceptance: store.rs defines the EventStore trait, async, bound Send + Sync + 'static.

append(&self, workflow_id: &WorkflowId, events: &[Event], expected_seq: u64) -> Result<(), StoreError>, documented as atomic (all events apply or none) and returning SequenceConflict when the stored head does not equal expected_seq.

read_history(&self, workflow_id: &WorkflowId) -> Result<Vec<Event>, StoreError> returns events in sequence order, and an empty Vec (never NotFound) for a workflow with no recorded events — the first append with expected_seq 0 creates the workflow implicitly, so 'unknown' and 'empty history' are the same observable state.
list_active(&self) -> Result<Vec<WorkflowId>, StoreError> returns non-terminal workflows.
query(&self, filter: &WorkflowFilter) -> Result<Vec<WorkflowSummary>, StoreError> returns matching summaries.
read_history, list_active, and query never return StoreError::NotFound for absent workflows; NotFound is reserved for operations that target a must-exist workflow.
Trait method docs state the contract precisely, including the atomicity, sequence-guard, and empty-history-for-unknown semantics.

requirements.title: Durable timer methods and TimerEntry
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define TimerEntry and add schedule_timer and expired_timers to EventStore. schedule_timer SHALL persist a durable timer; expired_timers SHALL return timers due to fire as of a given instant.
acceptance: timer.rs defines TimerEntry { workflow_id: WorkflowId, timer_id: TimerId, fire_at: DateTime<Utc> } deriving the standard traits.
EventStore gains schedule_timer(&self, &WorkflowId, &TimerId, fire_at: DateTime<Utc>) -> Result<(), StoreError>.
EventStore gains expired_timers(&self, as_of: DateTime<Utc>) -> Result<Vec<TimerEntry>, StoreError> returning timers whose fire_at <= as_of.
Docs state that scheduling a timer is part of the same logical transaction as the TimerStarted event the engine appends.
The trait remains object-safe (usable as Arc<dyn EventStore>).

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement the trait — InMemoryStore lands in AC-006.
SHALL NOT depend on beamr, the engine, or any storage library (libSQL, Postgres).
SHALL NOT split timers into a separate trait; they live on EventStore per design decision D6.
verification: EventStore is object-safe — a compile test constructs an Arc<dyn EventStore> (against a trivial stub or deferred to AC-006).
No #[allow], #[expect], or #[ignore] directives in new code.

checklist.text: StoreError is a closed enum with SequenceConflict, NotFound, Backend, and Serialization variants.
EventStore is an async trait that is Send + Sync + 'static.
append accepts an expected sequence number, applies all events atomically, and returns SequenceConflict if the stored head does not match.
read_history returns the complete event history for a workflow in sequence order.
list_active returns the WorkflowIds of all non-terminal workflows.
query returns WorkflowSummary values matching a WorkflowFilter.
schedule_timer persists a durable timer and expired_timers returns timers due to fire as of a given instant.

stories.text: As the engine, I want to list active workflows so that on startup I know which workflows to replay and resume.
As a store author, I want a single clear EventStore trait so that there is exactly one reasonable way to implement a backend correctly.
As a store author, I want to implement the trait without depending on the engine so that my backend crate stays decoupled.
As a worker, I want to depend on the domain types without pulling in the store trait so that my worker binary stays small and decoupled.

constraints.text: lib.rs/mod.rs are declarations and re-exports only.
aion-core and aion-store are leaf crates — they depend on no other Aion crate. Structural; must hold.
Every type that crosses the wire or the store derives Serialize + Deserialize (these are data types, not credentials).
WorkflowStatus is always derivable from event history; no independent status mutation.
InMemoryStore must pass the same behavioural test suite future durable backends are held to — it is the oracle, so it must be correct.
Identifiers are newtypes; no bare Uuid or String in public signatures where an ID is meant.

resolved_context.intention: Define the vocabulary every other part of Aion speaks: the event types, identifiers, workflow status, error taxonomy, and the persistence contract. When the engine records an activity completion, a client lists running workflows, a store persists history, or a worker reports a result, they all use the types defined here. The types must make illegal states unrepresentable — a completed workflow cannot also be running, an activity result cannot exist without a preceding schedule. Nothing here depends on beamr, the engine, networking, or any storage backend. This is the still point at the centre: pure types and one trait, so the rest of Aion can be built against stable contracts.
